title: A history of Y Combinator — from eight companies in a Cambridge apartment in 2005 to the AI-forward batch of 250 running this spring
dek: Y Combinator is twenty-one years old. It was invented by four people in Cambridge, moved to Mountain View, lost its bi-coastal shape, handed off from PG to Sam Altman to Geoff Ralston to Garry Tan, survived a COVID remote era, and is now the default first stop for AI founders on earth. Every accelerator in the world is a variation on the batch model YC ran for the first time in summer 2005.
body:
**The shortest honest version first.** YC is a startup accelerator founded in Cambridge, Massachusetts in March 2005 by Paul Graham, Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell. The founders invented the batch-accelerator model (small investment + three-month program + demo day, co-located) and it became the template every other accelerator copied. Roughly 5,000+ companies have been through the program; aggregate portfolio value is comfortably north of $600B. The batch model, the SAFE, the essays, Hacker News, and Demo Day as institution are all YC originals.

**2005 — Cambridge, summer, eight companies.** The first batch ran June–August 2005, called the Summer Founders Program, out of an office in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Eight companies. Six thousand dollars per founder. Twelve weeks ending in a pitch session to a small group of angels. Among the eight: Loopt (Sam Altman's location-sharing startup), Reddit (Steve Huffman + Alexis Ohanian), Infogami (Aaron Swartz — later merged into Reddit in November 2005 to form the company that became Reddit Inc.), and a handful of others that didn't make it. The format invented that summer — co-located batch, tiny investment, ten-minute partner meetings, weekly Tuesday dinners with outside speakers, a demo day at the end — is basically intact from then to now.

**Where the name comes from.** The Y combinator (`λf.(λx.f(x x))(λx.f(x x))`) is a fixed-point operator in lambda calculus — the function that lets you write recursive functions in a language without explicit self-reference. Graham, a Lisp programmer who wrote his dissertation-era work in Common Lisp and had already built Viaweb on it, chose the name as a reference to the idea of a function that produces other functions: an organization that produces startups. Also, it was memorable and unclaimed.

**2005–2008 — the bi-coastal years.** Original plan: Summer batches in Cambridge, Winter batches in Mountain View. Paul Graham and Jessica Livingston lived in Cambridge, but the money and the technical talent were in the Valley. Winter 2006 was the first Mountain View batch. Notable early grads across this period: Justin.tv (W2007, which pivoted into Twitch), Scribd (S2006), Weebly (W2007), Wufoo (W2006), Dropbox (S2007 — Drew Houston famously applied solo, got in, added Arash Ferdowsi later), and Airbnb (W2009, after being rejected the previous batch and barely surviving on credit-card debt and cereal-box revenue until YC).

**2008 — consolidation to Mountain View.** The East Coast batches ended. YC moved entirely to Pioneer Way in Mountain View. Batch size grew gradually: roughly 20 companies in 2008, ~40 in 2010, ~60 in 2012, ~80 by 2014. The investment deal evolved from the original $6K/founder to a $20K-for-~6% structure, with Yuri Milner's Start Fund (2010) adding a separate uncapped $150K to every YC company for a few years running — the first time a batch effect became a fundraising primitive.

**2007–2014 — the essay era, Hacker News, the curriculum going public.** Hacker News launched in 2007 on PG's own Arc Lisp dialect (originally called Startup News, renamed Hacker News early on) and became the discussion forum for the technical-founder demographic — an editorial project as much as a product. Graham's essays — 'Do Things That Don't Scale,' 'Default Alive or Default Dead,' 'Startups = Growth,' 'Before the Startup,' 'How to Start a Startup,' 'Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule,' 'The Hardware Hacker's Guide' — published on paulgraham.com through this era — became the de facto curriculum. Any founder anywhere could read them for free. This is an underrated part of why YC's model scaled culturally faster than its cohort did: the playbook was public.

**2013 — the SAFE.** YC's general counsel Carolynn Levy wrote the first Simple Agreement for Future Equity and published it open-source. SAFEs replaced convertible notes as the dominant early-stage instrument within about two years. This is arguably YC's most-copied innovation after the batch model itself — the legal primitive that cheap early-stage fundraising runs on, worldwide. A founder in Lagos or Sao Paulo raising a pre-seed today is almost certainly on a SAFE, almost certainly authored in Levy's original text with light modifications, almost certainly unaware of the provenance.

**February 2014 — PG steps back, Altman takes over.** Paul Graham announces Sam Altman (S2005 alum, Loopt founder, then 28) will become president. Jessica Livingston stays active as a partner. PG remains involved as an advisor but stops running operations. The handoff was deliberate succession, not a crisis — Graham had telegraphed for years that he wanted to write, not run an institution.

**2014–2019 — the Altman era, the scaling.** Under Altman: batches grow from ~80 to ~150 to ~200. YC Continuity Fund (2015) launches to invest in YC companies' later rounds — capturing the power-law upside YC had been giving away to downstream VCs. YC Research (2015) is created as a non-profit research arm; Altman co-founds OpenAI in December 2015 as a separate non-profit with Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others, with some personnel and ideological overlap with YC Research. The standard investment deal climbs to $120K for 7%, later to $150K MFN SAFE. Startup School (2017) goes fully online and free — an 8-week course anyone on earth can take without applying to YC. International batches begin. The Altman tenure is when YC becomes unambiguously the top-tier seed program globally.

**March 2019 — Altman leaves for OpenAI full-time.** Geoff Ralston (a longtime YC partner, ex-Yahoo Mail GM, author of the Ralston Scale for startup-fundraising readiness) becomes president. Altman remains a YC partner emeritus but his operating attention shifts entirely to OpenAI. The broader tech market is in its late-ZIRP phase — money is cheap, batch sizes keep growing.

**March 2020 — COVID, fully remote.** The W2020 batch finished in-person; S2020 was the first fully remote batch, conducted over Zoom. Remote persisted through 2021 and into 2022. Batch sizes ballooned — some batches topped 400 companies. The loose consensus from founders who went through both eras: fully remote diluted the shape. Harder to bump into founders at the espresso machine, harder to run the Tuesday dinners, less of the 'one room' social pressure that made the in-person version work. Some batch-mates never met each other.

**November 2022 — Tan announced, January 2023 — Tan takes over.** Garry Tan (S2008 alum, Posterous co-founder, ex-Initialized Capital partner) is announced as YC president on November 30, 2022, and takes over in January 2023. Tan's agenda, stated publicly: shrink batches back toward Altman-era size, return to in-person in Mountain View, restore the weekly Tuesday dinners, lean explicitly into AI. Batch size is pulled back to roughly 250–300 from the 400+ inflation peak. Tan is also more publicly political than any previous YC president, particularly on San Francisco governance — a departure in tone that reads as deliberate brand work.

**2023–2026 — the AI pivot.** Under Tan, YC's messaging becomes unambiguously AI-forward. Demo Day after Demo Day is majority AI-focused — GPT wrappers, foundation-model fine-tuning, agent frameworks, AI-native vertical SaaS. Some observers argue the shift is overdone (too many variations on the same wrapper pattern); others that it's the right call for the moment (if you're not building with LLMs in 2024-2026, you're building with obsolete tools). Batches stabilize around 250. Standard deal evolves to the $500K structure: $125K SAFE for 7% + $375K MFN SAFE uncapped. The program format itself remains: three months, Tuesday dinners, office hours, demo day.

**What YC actually invented.** Five things, in rough order of cultural weight:

1. **The batch accelerator.** Small investment + three-month program + demo day, co-located. Every other accelerator on earth is a variation on this — Techstars, 500 Global, Seedcamp, Entrepreneur First, all downstream of the YC shape.

2. **The SAFE.** The legal primitive powering cheap early-stage fundraising, globally. Saved founders a billion-plus in collective legal fees.

3. **The essay as curriculum.** Graham's essays made the playbook public and portable. Any founder anywhere could read them for free and adopt the operating system.

4. **Hacker News.** The discussion forum for the technical-founder demographic for almost two decades and counting. Still the top referrer to most serious tech-content sites.

5. **Demo Day as institution.** The batch's structural climax, copied everywhere — every accelerator now has one.

**The playbook, compressed.** What YC teaches, in short:

- Make something people want.
- Talk to users. Then talk to more users.
- Do things that don't scale (early).
- Launch early; iterate in public.
- Grow ~5–7% week over week.
- Ramen profitable is not just a number, it's a state of mind.
- Default alive beats default dead.
- The goal of the first twelve weeks is PMF, not perfection.
- Fundraising is a distraction until it isn't; close fast and get back to work.

**The alumni graph.** The real moat. Top alumni by exit or current valuation: Stripe (W2009), Airbnb (W2009), Instacart (S2012), Coinbase (S2012), DoorDash (S2013), Reddit (S2005), Dropbox (S2007), Gusto (W2012), Twitch (via Justin.tv W2007), Ginkgo Bioworks (S2014), Cruise (W2014), Brex (W2017), Deel (W2019), Rippling (founders are YC alumni via Zenefits), PagerDuty (S2010), Razorpay (W2015), Rappi (W2016), Zapier (S2012). Five-thousand-plus companies with every batch adding to the network effect.

**Why it still works after twenty-one years.** The batch model was a bet that peer-learning + concentrated social pressure + a deadline (demo day) + a tiny amount of money would produce outsized outcomes. That bet has held. The specific configuration has changed (name, geography, batch size, partner count, explicit AI focus, remote-vs-in-person oscillation), but the underlying shape — cohort + short program + demo day + essay-based curriculum + alumni network — is stable. Stability is the compounding mechanism: every batch adds to the alumni graph, which is the single largest network of technical founders on earth. That's the moat. Other accelerators can copy the format; they can't copy the graph.

**The thread to this site.** PointCast runs the same primitive at a different resolution. YC is 'ship a company in twelve weeks.' PointCast is 'ship a block in twenty-four hours.' Same cadence, different unit. Mike's S2014 batch is one dot on the arc above — block 0399 is its stamp. This block is the larger arc the dot sits on. The founder-formation that YC's first batch invented in summer 2005 is the same formation running in El Segundo at block-per-day resolution now. A block is a miniature demo day; the batch-mates are every other block on the grid; the user is every other founder reading the feed. The unit got smaller, the loop got faster, the structure held.
author: mh+cc
source: Mike chat 2026-04-21 23:50 PT: 'build out the history of ycombinator.' cc drafted from general knowledge + YC public record (paulgraham.com essays, ycombinator.com/about, public announcements of president transitions). Facts hedged where dates or batch sizes are approximate. Author = mh+cc.
